original_abstract:
This paper introduces the ReferItGame, a novel two-player crowdsourcing game designed to collect and verify natural language referring expressions for objects in real-world photographs. By structuring data collection as a game where one player describes an object and another must localize it via a click, the authors efficiently generate a large-scale dataset containing over 130,000 expressions for nearly 100,000 distinct objects. This corpus significantly surpasses previous datasets in both scale and complexity, moving beyond simple geometric or laboratory settings to cluttered, natural scenes. 

The authors perform an extensive analysis of the dataset, uncovering key insights such as how object category influences the selection of attributes (e.g., color, size, and location) and the identification of "entry-level" naming conventions used by humans. Building on these empirical findings, the paper proposes a new optimization-based model for referring expression generation. This model jointly determines which attributes to include and their corresponding values by integrating visual recognition models with category-specific linguistic priors. Experimental evaluations across three diverse test sets demonstrate that the proposed model effectively mimics human-like referential behavior in complex environments.
humanized_abstract:
This work presents the ReferItGame, a two-player crowdsourcing game to collect and validate referring expressions (e.g. "person at the wheel" in the image on the left) for objects depicted in photos. The ReferItGame turns the data collection process into a game where the describer player explicates the position of an object in an image for the locater player who tries to click on it. This results in a very large-scale dataset with over 130K expressions describing almost 100K distinct objects. This new dataset is much larger and more diverse than existing datasets (which were usually defined with much simpler settings and often focused on few distinctive geometric features).

In this paper, we extensively analyze the dataset to discover the impacts of object category on the choice of attributes and other facts such as the basic level naming (entry-level) of referring expressions used by humans. Then, we develop a new optimization-based model, referred to as OptER, to generate high-quality referring expressions. The goal of the optimization process is to decide which attributes to mention and what their values to use, by incorporating object detection models as well as the category-specific linguistic priors. The evaluation over three distinct datasets supports the feasibility of OptER in imitating the unique referring expression behaviors exhibited by humans in various scenes.
